CURRENT AFFAIRS,

The Scotch Times of Dublin is enthusiastic in the
cause of working up an anti-Irish fecling. It must be
rather galling fo the Scotch Times to find that the
material at hand is not superabundant. Our roaders
well remember that it worked some brawl, or alleged
brawl, between some civilians and some Tommy Atkins’s
in Donegal for all it was worth—and a great deal more.
In a recent issue the Scotch Times devoted its first
leading article to a letter of some anonymous person
living in Cork—perhaps, one of the Arnotts—who
*wrote to the Forger in London by way of ‘‘ telling on”’
‘the Cork County Council to the Big Brother. This
anonymous tatler calls himself a “Helpless Rate-
payer.””  The tale that he relates partly concerns the
appointment of O’Donovan Rossa to an humble job
carrying a salary of £150 a yoar. According to this
story-carrier to the Big Brother, Rossa was allowed to
We do not know the
particulars, and we would be sorry to accept as facts
.gtatements made by anti-Irish tale-bearers to the Big

Brother’s paper, the Forger. We may say, that if
there is any looseness concerning the fulfilling of the
duties of the position to which Rossa was appointed, no
people would condemn it more strongly than we would
condemn it. The Scotch Times writes a leading article
about “ Helpless Ratepayer’s’’ letter in the Forger.
The anti-Irish drowning man naturally catches at a
straw. We have seen the Mail, Lord Bung Ardilaun’s
paper, trying to work a politico-financial scare concern-
ing the fall of the so-called Bank of Ireland shares,
when probably the explanation of the drop is that a lot
of the shareholders of the Bank have received calls of
£6 per share on their holdings in the financial muddle-
heap by the name of the ‘‘ National Assurance Co.”’;
and no doubt the fact that some of the directors of the
muddle-heap that has inflicted a call of £240,000 on its
shareholders, are also directors of the so-called Bank
of Ireland, has not tended to strengthen confidence in
that institution. Men who muddle an assurance com-
pany’s affairs could drive a bank over a precipice too,
we suppose.

Well, the Scotch Times tried its political “ yellow
journal ”’ hand at the Rossa appointment by the Cork
County Council. Why does not the Scotch Times turn
its attention to Mr. Fawcett-of-the many-jobs, of Rath-
mines, who had his railings repaired by the workmen in
the pay of the District Council, and who paid the Dis-
trict Council for the use of its workmen? The Rath-
mines Council is a ““ saved *’ and Unionist preserve, and
its rates are only 10s. 6d. in the £, and ‘‘ Helpless
ratepayers ’ like ourselves have no power.

Another complaint against Ireland is that one Mr.
J. J. Roycroft, Deputy County Surveyor to the Cork
County Council, was asked when it would be con-
venient for him to submit himself for examination in
Trish. We suppose if employees of County Councils in
Ireland were asked to qualify in the dialect spoken by
the Scotch Times’ Mr. Carlyle, it would be a sign of
enlightenment and progress!  The Scotch Times re-
marks: —‘“ With these opinions ‘ Helpless Ratepayer’
will find much sympathy among a large class of Irish-
men, who, being Unionists, are the principal ratepayers
in Ireland, and are, like himself, quite helpless.”” The
italics are ours! The ‘‘ principal ratepayers,’’ indeed ;
the principal spongers on Ireland. We have a recollec-
tion that years ago we wrote an article that tended to
show that the principal Unionists in one sense paid no
rates or taxes, for they have nearly a monopoly of the
well-paid patronage jobs that are supported out of the
rates and taxes of this overtaxed country. They live
on the taxes in fact. Rossa’s £150 a year is a small
thing in comparison with the thousands that the Sour-
face judges, the doting Trinity College people and the
rest draw for doing, frequently, worse than nothing in
this country. The Scotch Times should send a few of
the Arnott’s, Lockyer, Carlyle, a sclection of their
sourest reporters—and some of them are sour we make
no doubt—and if they could discover that ‘‘ Roman
Catholic Farmer *’ of Killaloe fame, and pack the whole
lot over to the white shores of Albion, on the stump
by way of ‘“enlightening’’ the Big Brother, perhaps
they would do great service to the cause of “‘the
hempire ’’ and themsclves. We shrewdly suspect that
at the back of the Scotch Times’ head is the fear that
the Irishising of Ireland might knock some of the gilt off
itself, and it might, as a consequence, not get so many
advertisements.  We admit that the future of the
““Irish” Times gives cause for concern; a DAILY
Leaper would soon affect the market value of its shares.
We have already scen that the Scoteh Times has conbri-

buted two guineas to the Irish Language Fund, though
it loses no opportunity to attack the Irish movement.
If Treland developed as Ireland the Scotch Zimes might
feel compelled for canny business reasons to become
Irish! Alas, poor Carlyle! Scots wha hae would feel
limp under such distressing circumstances.

A student at the Belfast Technical Classes took it
into her patriotic head to push the sale and popularise
.the use of Trish:made thread in crochet work. A lady
official at the Belfast Technical Classes, however, told
her that if she used Irish thread she used it ab hor own
risk. This did not intimidate the patriotic student, as
we do not suppose it entered her head to use thread at
anyone eclse’s risk but her own. The official told her
that the Department recommended English thread for
crochet. How is this, we wonder? What particular
official —imported or otherwisc—was responsible for
advising English thread for the crochet work at the
Technical Classes of Belfast, a town in a district in
which thread is manufactured.

The patriotic student ignored the advice of the De-
partment in favour of English thread. She went to a
firm of manufacturers of thread, Messrs. Wm. Barbour
and Souns, Ltd., for samples of thread. Messrs. Barbour
referred her to agents of theirs, Messrs. Robinson and
Cleaver, Ltd., and at the latter place the student got
some samples of Irish thread for work in the Technical
Classes, even though the Department advise English
thread. -

In due time the student got a bill for tenpence for
the samples supplied her by Messrs. Robinson & Cleaver,
Ltd. The student allowed the firm to use up threepenny
stamps sending her bills for the 10d. On receipt of the
third bill the lady, who was in no great humour for
going to further efforts to push the sale of Irish thread,
wrote to Messrs. Wm. Barbour & Sons, Ltd., the manu-
facfurers, concerning the matter. They wrote back:—
‘“ Hilden Thread Mills, Lisburn.—Madam—We are in
receipt of yours of ——, addressed to our Belfast office,
and we cannot understand why you have been charged
for samples. In order to investigate the matter we
would feel obliged by your returning us the Invoice sent
you for these samples. We enclose herewith stamped
addressed envelope for favour of your reply.”

The enthusiastic student who was anxious, despite
the advice of the Department in favour of English
thread, to push the sale of Irish thread, sent the
Invoice to Messrs. Barbour & Sons, Ltd., as requested.
Messrs. Barbour & Sons, Ltd., thereupen, sent the fol-
lowing further letter: —‘ Madam—We are in receipt
of yours of the 7th inst., handing us Invoice you re-
ceived from Messrs. Robinson & Cleaver, Ltd., Belfast.
Beeing this is a matter between yourself and Messrs.
Robinson & Cleaver we regret we cannot interfere in the
matter. We handed your enquiry to this firm to attend
to, seeing that they are a wholesale firm and could
supply you on as equally advantageous terms as we
could.” ~ Well, notwithstanding all the ‘ seeing’’ there
is in the letter, we don’t see any sense in it. In the
first letter Messrs. Barbour & Sons, Ltd., declared—
‘“We cannot understand why you have been charged
for samples,” and in the second letter, written only
three days afterwards, it appears that ‘‘secing ’’ this
and ““seeing ’’ that they cannot interfere. Well, we
suppose the lady will pay Messrs. Robinson & Cleaver,
Ltd., the 10d. for samples, and we are not surprised to
hear that she has made up her mind that this is the
first and last time she will go out of her way to patrioti-
cally attempt to extend the use of thread made in Ire-
land by Messrs. William Barbour & Sons, Ltd., of
Lisburn, who, we note, write on paper water-marked
‘“ Conqueror, London.”’

bacon factory in Roscrea intend giving the contract for
the building, machinery, etc., to a Scotch agent. Wo
understand that no estimates have heen asked from
local or other Irish firms. ‘The contracts, we hear, aro

Mr. John Iamilton, Hon. Sec. Provisional Com-
mittee of the Feis Connacht, St. Jarlath’s Place, Tuam,
requests us to publish the following circular which has
been sent each secretary of Coipoe Ceanncap and Feis
Committee in the Province: —‘“ At a meeting of Gaelic
Leaguers of Connacht held in Galway during the Feis
week, Father Crehan, Riverstown, presiding, it was
decided that a Connacht Feis be held in 1907. A small
Provisional Committee was appointed to act in con-
junction with representatives from each Coisde
Ceanntair and Feis Committee in the Province to form
a Provincial Committee to carry the project through.
I am directed to request that your Committee will
appoint two representatives who will act on the Pro-
vincial Committee. The first meeting will be held at
Ryan’s Hotel, Claremorris, at 12 o’clock, noon, on
Thursday, 27th instant, and as very important matters
will be discussed I should be glad if your representatives
would attend on that day.”” Mr. Hamilton asks us to
state that if secretaries of a Coipoe Ceanncaip or Feis
Committee should not have received a copy he would
be obliged if they would, on sceing the circular in the
LEADER, communicate at once with him.

A correspondent, in the course of a letter on The
Gaelic Leaguo and the Outer World,”” writes:— The

last and only objection now left to the opponents of tho -

In proof of their assertion they point to the fact that,
although the League has now been established some

classos and festivals—is now as silent in public life in
Treland as it was before the League was started.”” He
urges that Irish speakers should use the language more

The official figures which a contributor in last week’s
LrADER seb out- showing the valuation per head of tho
various counties in Ireland are instructive, and some of
our readers in England might draw attention to them
in the English Press. It will tako a long time to get
the information into the hoads of England's Faithful
Garrison in this country, for no people are so deaf as
they who do not wish to hear, nor so blind as those who
do not wish to see. For instance, Arnott’s paper, the
“ZIrish >’ T'imes, in a leading articlo in Saturday’s issuo
refers to Ulster as “ the province where tho bulk of the
nation’s industry and prosperity is concentrated.”” Thom
must be a liar, then! . At an Orange meeting at Arti-
clave Mr. H. T. Barrie, M.P., for North Derry—we
understand Mr. Barrie is a native Scotchman—delivered
an harangue in the course of which he referred te “ Pro-
testant and Prosperous Ulster,”” and “ Prosperous and
Unionist Ulster.””  Might we suggest to the Irish
Unionist Alliance that they should take a libel action
against Thom’s Directory at once’

t would seem that to testimonials to policemen and
bank clerks there is no end. In Macroom, of all places
in the world, they have presented a testimonial to a bank
clerk. The bank clerk, whose name is Haines, is a
comparative stranger in Macroom. However, he has
the economic advantage of being one of the “Saved,”
and we understand that he is a rather stiff man
in the bank. The people of Macroom, we believe, could
not see what claim the clerk had on them that he should
receive a purse of sovereigns; but what business have
the mere people to an opinion of their own? Is it not
the inalienable right of tame “Idolators’’ to bow and
scrape before “ Saved *’ clerks in banks? The chairman
of the local branch of the United Irish League was one
of those concerned gathering up the purse of sovereigns
for one of those “persecuted '’ outcasts in the South
and West of Ireland whose sad plight is served up hot
by the Orange tittle-tattlers to the Big Brother by way
of fighting not only Home Rule, but even milk and
water Deovolution. In a leading article in Arnott's
“Irish” Times of Saturday wo read:—“Ho (Walter
Long, M.P.) expressed an absolute confidence in the
fidelity of the Unionists of Ulster to their scattered
fellow-loyalists in the South and West.”” And one of
the “scattered fellow-loyalists ’—we assume that the
clerk, Mr. Haines, being “Saved "’ is also “ loyal ”’—
gets a purse of sovereigns in Macroom, the chair being
taken by a  persecutor ”’ by the name of Mr. O’Leary,
J.P., a member of the County Council. Mr. Foley,
Chairman of the United Irish Leaguo that is supposed
particularly to have its knife in the “ scattored fellow-
loyalists in the South and West,”’ assisted, and so, we
are surprised to hear, did Dr. O‘Dquoghuq, a former
Chairman of the  persecuting”’ United Irish Leaguo.
There was a concert in connection with the “ momentous
occasion ”’ of presenting sovereigns to this clerk in
Macroom, and no doubt Mr. Bung did nob lose.

A special meeting of the Education Sub-Committco
was held to consider, in accordance with tho instructions
from the Ard-Fheis, “the drafting ‘_of a scheme for
bringing the genoral body of tho children of Ireland
into intimate touch with the work and teaching of the
Gaelic League.” Suggestions as to the foundation of
a hoys’ brigade on Gaelic League lines wero rqeelveltl
from Limmn de Roiste, Cor. Aflier much discussion, it
was decided to begin by an experiment on a small scale,

and to endeavour to start, in a hall or large room some-

The “ Komik Kork Eggsaminer’’ won’t have Presi-
dont Roosevelt’s new spelling reform. It wants a local
University for Cork at present. The following cutting
from a LEADING article in that highly intecllectual organ
will show the need there is for even primary education
on thoe staff. Is it that the “ Komik’' has a spelling
reform of its own }— It is dificult to see how Mr. Bal-
four, or anybody else concerned, can any longer obect
to the publjication of everp décument which will contri-
bute to the elucidation of the truth. There is a pos-
sulity that tho documents may put a better compleion
on the case, and they really cannot, make it lookz much
worso.””  The italics are ours.  “ They really cannot
make it lookx much worse!”” Can they?

In the printed forms of application for positions such
as examinerships under the Intermediate Board, appli-
cants have to answer the question—What is your reli-
gion?  This, we submit, is putting the thing much
plainer than even the Bigot Great Northern Railway’s
dodge for the exclusion of “Idolators.” No doubt it
is pure academic curiosity that induces the.stalwarts
from the Robber University that run the Board with
the help of a miserable tail of tames to pry into the
religion of applicants for examinerships and the like
posts. It would, no doubt, be convenient to endow
with the public money cureless parsons from the Spell-
ing Bee, and sons of the distinguished host of the Free
and Accepted Masons of Ireland. The recent results
give an indication of the relative proportion of “Idola-
tors”’ and of “Saved’ among the Intermediate stu-
dents; bub as the Board is constituted, it is inevitable
that the Souper University will carry off the main por-
tion of the loot. The inspector staff of the “ National’
Board is another body that requires looking after.
There is a majority of “ Saved ’’ inspectors on the staff.
The entire atmosphere of the Board is, we have reason
to know, becoming daily more and more assimilated to
that of the Intermediate Board, and of the Souper Uni-
versity itself. Inferior “ Saved’ teachers get promo-
tion reports while the “ Idolatrous’ teachers next door,
who are superior to them in every way, not only are
refused promotion reports, but often run the visk of
being dismissed for ineficiency. ~We have it on the
authority of a well-seasoned official that the proportion
of merit that spells promotion or increaso of salary for
a “Saved ”’ and an “Idolatrous’’ teacher is as 1 to 4.

Registration business is now in full swing in Rath-
mines, the notorious district run on two tickets by
Unionists in which nearly all the better paid jobs are
held by “Saved,” and in which the rates are 10s. 6d.
in tho £. Nationalists have evidently been objected
to wholesale ; wo rocently drew attention to the fact that
Mr. P. J. O'Driscoll got two objections. Miss Mary
Leo, of 43 Grosvenor Square, Rathmines, was placed on
the rogister in 1904. Last year sho was put on without
objection. This yoar her claim has been objected to
though her claim to bo on the register is the same as
1904, when she was first pub on, and the same as in
1905, when sho was not objected to. The objector is
one Thomas T. Ladd, a gentleman whose name is known
oven boyond thoe limits of Rathmines. In the course of
tho notice of objection we read :—“ Take notice that I
object to your name being retained in the List (or Local
Government Supplement to the List) of Voters for the

Registration Unit of Rathmines and Rathgar West, and
to the omission of your Name from the Corrupt and
Illegal Practices List.”” The italics are ours. Who is
this Mr. Ladd who objects to this lady’s name not
appearing on the Corrupt and Illegal Practices List?
Mr. Thomas Theodore Ladd, our readers will romem-
ber, is a gontleman who was committed to jail about
two years ago in connection with South Dublin registra-
tion business.

There was a long report of the Ardagh Diocesan
_Syuod of the Church of England’s Faithful Garrison
in Ireland in the Scotch Zimes of Dublin. The Bishop’s
address was all about money, money, money. Idis
Lordship, whatever else he may lack, has the ‘‘economic
sense ’’ well developed. Ie wailed over the passing of
the landlords, whose unchristian exactions and neglect
of the duties attaching to property have brought such
ruin and havoc on this country.  This particular Bishop
of the Church of England’s Faithful Garrison in Ire-
land, in the course of his financial address to the
Ardagh Diocesan Synod, said:—

_ It was well known that a large fraction of tho ordinary

income of the Church consisted of landlords’ subscription to

assessment,  He was informed that about half the assces-
ment in that diocese camo from that source.  He dared
say it was the rule in most dioccses except Dublin. Well,
shese subscribers were obliged to give up the ownership of
their property for a sum of money which would rarely yicld
them their already reduced incomes. The value of an acre

of land was not affected by its tramsfer from one man to

another, but the point at which the Church was touched was

that the chief value of the land, and its actual possession,
passed away from a friend to one who, in nine cases out of
ten, was non-friendly.

This Bishop of the English Garrison Church went
on:—

TWhen men were proprietors they felt that their rights were
conditioned by dutics. They had a patriarchal regard for
those who dwelt on their estates, and secing that their
Protestant tenants were few, they thought it right to assist
them to maintain the faith of their fathers. The boast of

the agitator was that landlordism was destroyed; therefore,
the help afforded to the Church by that class could not Le
continued.

The italics are ours, and we have no doubt they will
add to the gaiety of our readers. While the
Church of England’s Faithful Garrison in Ireland,
Bishop of Kilmore, Elphin, and Ardagh, was be-
moaning the loss, or expected loss, from the great pest
of Ireland, the landlords, did he pass a thought on the
welfare of the souls of these landlords? Landlords, in
general, have been a curse to the country, they have
helped to ruin it, to degrade it, to impoverish it. Now
men who have all these things upon their conscience
are in danger of having a hot time of it for their souls
at a future date; yet the souls of these landlords was
not the theme of the Bishop’s harangue, it was their
money, the probable stoppage of their subscription that
apparently chiefly concerned the Bishop. His lordship
has the ‘“ economic sense,”’ however it may be with his
spiritual sense.

The President of the Ardagh Diocesan Synod of Eng-
land’s Garrison Church is not sorry for the emigration
that is going on; he, in this matter, is, of course, more
enlightened than “‘the surpliced ruffians of Maynooth.”
His lordship said: —

They (the “Saved”) were diminizhing in number, especi-
ally in the South and West, the decrease IJcm‘[; }nnmly duo

to emigration. Personally, he had not _the dislike to emi-

gration that was frequently expressed by Roman Catholio

priests and political agitators. Peoplo would go whero they
could best provide for themselves and their fmmlxqs, and no
persuasion could keep them back. As regards their dopant-
ing people, their lives would not be wasted if they helped

to build up Greater Britain in distant lands, and if they oar-

ried with them the principles of the Church of Ireland the

cause of truth in the world would not euffer.

The italics are ours.

Next day the Synod of Elphin was held, and the
Bishop, as president of the Syndd, delivered a violent
harangue. So closely are the ‘“saved ’ identified with
the Unionist political party in this country that this

We have before us the prospectus of the Municipal

Needless to say, it is almost like looking for a needle in
a bundle of hay to search for the few ¢ Idolators’

brethren in the North-east corner have a hard time of

It is anticipated that the Irish National Trade Mark
will be ready for issue early in November. At a meet-
ing of the Irish Industrial Development Association
(Incorporated) the conditions on which the Trade Mark
will be granted were discussed. An agreement is to be
drafted, the chief clause of which will deal with the
Council’s right to prosecute anyone found guilty of
wrongfully using Trade Mark; the power to withdraw
the use of same from any firm acting in such a manner;
and that users of the Trade Mark must either attach
their name or else a distinguishing mark to all packages
to which Trade Mark is affixed. The Council decided
that overy firm to which permission is given to use
Trade Mark must pay (£2 2s.) two guineas per annum
o the Association for the use of the mark, except in
tho caso of very small industries, such as struggling lace
industries; in respect to which Council decided that,
provided it is satisfied that it would be a serious drain
on the resources of such an industry, it will reserve to
itsolf tho right to reduce the chargo to 10s, per annum.
Manufacturers should make application immediately,
addressed tg t.he Hon. Sec., Irish Industrial Develop-
mont Association (Incorporated), 13 Marlboro’ Street,
Cork, for the uso of Trade Mark.

The performances of the “ Idolatrous’’ boys at this
year’s Intermediate explains, though it doesnot justify,
the anxiety of the directors of the Great Northern Bigot
Railway to retain the powers of giving marks for an
airy subject of the natwre of “ manners.”” For boys
springing from the section of the people who only pos-
sess one-fourth or some such insignificant portion of the
“intelligence *’ in this country the “Idolatrous’’ lads
did very well, and the “Idolatrous’’ girls likewise did
well. Out of £5,142 awarded in all grades to boys,
students of “Idolatrous’’ schools and colleges won

£3,652, and students of “Saved ”’  schools,
notwithstanding all their pampering, only pulled
off £1,490. What is more significant whilst

the Papist schools increased their gross prizo results—
though the total amount distributed was only £5,142
as against £5,537 last year—over last year, the total
of the Protestant schools fell from £1,901 last year to
£1,490 this year, a big and ominous drop for the party
with nearly all the ‘‘intelligence!”” Counting heads, the
pupils of the boys’ Catholic schools, in the matter
of Exhibitions and prizes, beat those of the boys’
Protestant schools by over three to one. No wonder
the clerks of the Royal Dublin Society, the Provincial
Bank, and other places are not selected by open compe-
titive examination.

St. Colman’s College, Fermoy, did very well again
this year. Notwithstanding that they suffered in a few
instances from what, in billiards, is called *“ hard lines,”’
one student in the Junior Grade got more marks than
fifteen Exhibitioners in another section in the same grade,
and yet he got no Exhibition, and a Middle Grade boy
was hardly treated a'so. IHowever, the college did
practically as well as last year. The distinction of
having won first in Senior Grade Mathematical Course,
and highest marks in the grade in any course, is one of

- which St. Colman’s may be justly proud. In the matter
of Irish St. Colman’s have obtained more passes than
oven last year. Seventy-one students passed, and thirty-
five got honours in Irish.

THE TRANSFERRING OF THE LAND.

OFTEN wonder if those who advocate the study of

Irish history confine their enthusiasm to the past,
or whether they would cast ever a glance on the his-
tory of our own times. The past appeals to the imagi-
nation ; that there is no denying. Indeed, it appeals
s5 much to it that History, instead of becoming a study
or a science, becomes matter for oratory. Hence we
know all about what William the Third said he would
do to our woollen trade, all about the Wild Geese, and
Fontenoy, and other affairs of that kind, but we know
very little about the economic condition of rustic Ire-
land—a matter surely of not contemptible importance.
I would venture to say that the Report of the Estates
Commissioners, so recently published, contains matter
of much more moment to us to-day than anything that
was done “at the huts of Fontenoy.”’ But the bril-
liant charge of Lord Clare’s Dragoons at Fontenoy
strikes the imagination, whereas the difference between
“security "’ and “price’’ (a thing of far more conse-
quence to Ireland to-day) does not strike the imagina-
tion at all. It may be doubted if the popular mind
has yet thoroughly grasped the fact that the Estates
Commissioners” Report is a justification of Mr. Sexton
and Mr. Dillon, as against Mr. William O’Brien and
his friends of the “ moderate ’’ party; yot such is the
fact. It may be doubted, too, if our more imaginative-
minded students of History—in the form of mental cine-
matograph pictures, with limelight on them—have- yet
wakened up to the fact that through the agency of the
Land Commission, the Congested Districts Board and

the Department of Agriculture, more important changes .

are being wrought than any that were ever effected by
Irish heroism at Almanza or Cremona. Let ‘us, ‘how-
ever, do what the imaginative student of Irish history

will 2ot do; let us see how does Ireland stand in the .

prosaic, but far from unimportant, matter of land pur-
chase. The Report already referred to makes the matter
opportune, as it contains statistics of purchase under
the Wyndham Act up to the end of last March.

The first important step in the way of land purchase
was made possible by the Church Act of 1869, when
John Bright procured the insertion of a clause enabling
the glebe tonants to buy their holdings, three-fourths of
the purchase money being advanced by the Government.
6,057 tenants purchased their holdings under this Act
But these were all glebe tenants; the ordinary farmer
on lay estates remained as he was. Then came the im-
portant Land Act of 1870, which contained some pro-
visions for purchase. But the facilities were not great,
and only 877 tenants bought their holdings under this
Act. The purchase clauses of the great Act of 1881
wero even less effectual, as only 731 tenants purchased
under them. The real beginning of general purchase
was undor the Act of 1885, Some of us who are now
gotting on to middle age remember what happened in
that year. The Gladstone Ministry, after years of un-
popularity as regards Ireland and the Trish, was driven

from office by a combination of Tories and Nationalists

amid great jubilation of the Irish members and the

Churchill phalanx, The Tories took office, and held

their short stay on the ministerial benches they passed
the Ashbourne Act, which authorised the issue of
£5,000,000 on loan for land purchase in Ircland. The
five millions were soon taken up, and when the Tories
returned to power another five millions were issued.
Under the Ashbourne Acts (1885-1891) there was issued
in all £9,992,636 for land purchase, and 25,367 tenants
bought their holdings. Next came Mr. A. J. Balfour’s
complicated Act of 1891, under which £33,000,000 were
to be issued. This Act did not work so well, and it was
mended in 1896 by Mr. Gerald Balfour. Under these
Balfour Acts there were purchased 40,072 holdings, and
£11,542,814 were advanced for the purpose—not in
cash, indeed, for the landlord was paid in Land Stock
scrip.  The Wyndham Act of 1903 is (supposed to be)
 familiar matter of to-day.”’ According to the recent
Report, tho number of holdings for the purchase of
which advances have been made under this Act from
November 1, 1903, to March 31, 1906, was 21,716, for
which £9,855,046 was advanced. The total number of
agreements to purchase notified to the Estates Commis-
sioners was much greater than the twenty-one thousand
mentioned as having actually received advances. The
gross number was 97,245, and the total advances applied
for amounted to £35,275,831. Thus we see that out of
thirty-five millions applied for, less than ten have been
issued, and the arrears of advances amount to more than
twenty-five millions, while 75,529 tenants are waiting
for the money to buy with. In short, the Imperial
credit is not able to keep pace with the progress of
agreements for sale.

It would be interesting to try and determine what
proportion of the holdings of Ireland have been pur-
chased. To do this we should have to exclude the
uneconomic holdings and the plots under one acre,
which are presumably labourers’ gardens. The latter
we should exclude as not ordinary agricultural hold-
ings, and the former as not self-supporting holdings,
and consequently as not those which we can expect to
see purchased, save by the agency of the Congested Dis-
tricts Board, or some similar machinery. It is not too
easy to get a satisfactory basis of calculation to start
from. There are more holdings than occupiers, since
some farmers have more than one holding. Again, in
a table where the holdings are given according to size
wo find 543,649 occupiers; in one giving the valuation
—beginning with those “ Up to £4 ”’—we find 490,301
occupiers. Doubtless the differenco between the two
sets of figures consists of those whose plots are not
valued for taxation purposes. On the whole, it is
safest to take the larger figure, 543,649, and deduct
from it the number of occupiers of holdings under one
acre, 73,352, which leaves 470,297. There are esti-
mated to be 200,000 uneconomic holdings, mainly, of
course, in the West; deducting these we got 270,297 as
the legitimate self-supporting holdings of agricultural
Treland. Now let us sum up the amount of purchase
actually in process of effectuation : —

As things stand, 94,820 occupiers are out of the clutch
of landlordism, and 76,629 others are pretty sure to be

in the mattor of land purchase. As to tho uneconomic
holdings of the West, they will probably be dealt with
under whatover scheme is evolved as a result of the
report of tho Commission on the Congested Districts.
Up to the present the Congested Districts Board has
dealt with some 14,500 holdings, and, as Sir Antony
MacDonnell has put it, only the fringe of the question
has as yot beon touched. Still 14,500 is something;
added to the 94,820 occupiers already delivered from
landlordism, this would make 109,320. On the whole,
it may perhaps be said that land purchase stands at
about what the footballers would call “half time;’’
that is, outside the congested districts, for there it is
by no means “half time’’ yet. At all events, we see
that a great portion of the land of Ireland has changed
hands, and this fact, together with Catholic successes at
the Royal University and the Intermediate, gives us a
good reason for boasting that we are now the people of
wealth and intelligence. IyaAL.

TWENTY YEARS AFTER.

HE Irish people are at last beginning to take a
faint interest in their own future.  For two
months they have been gazing thoughtfully over the
railings of Trinity College, listening to the abuse, and
‘hoping it would come to a fight. But one afternoon
there came a stop-press, and now they have turned
round, and are looking in the other direction towards
the Bank of Ireland. It is only indirectly, indeed,
that they are interested in their own affairs. If Ire-
land were to get self-government it might make a change
in the geography of College Green, and that prospect is
so startling and so important that people are being
actually brought to consider the question of Irish
government as by a side-wind. It is a more interesting
proof of the profound effect of sentiment that in the
Home Rule (guestiou, as in the University Question,
bricks and mortar are found of more account than men
and institutions. The rumour that College Green would
once again be the home of the Irish representatives has
created a sensation where detailed schemes of govern-
ment, not at all unlikely to become law, have scarcely
been glanced at.

The recent meeting of the Directory of the United
Irish League and its decision to consider any scheme of
devolution on its merits, has also tended to make the
question of Irish government a living one. To some
that decision has come as a surprise. It has been looked
upon as a sudden and causeless abandonment of prin-
ciple on the part of the Irish Party. But, in reality, it
is but the logical conclusion of a long course of action.
If the Irish Party were resolved to reject anything less
than Home Rule, why did they agree with the Liberals,
when still in opposition, on a non-Home Rule amend-
ment to the address? Why did they support them, un-
pledgod to Home Rule, or even pledged against i, at .
tho Genoral Election? Why have they since assisted
them in the House of Commons? Why, in truth, if
thoy seck nothing but Home Rule should they remain
at all in a Parliament where they have clearly no pre-
sent chance of getting it? The decision to accept the
half-loaf was taken not a week, but many months ago,
and the Irish members will, in truth, be as much respon-
sible for any measure of devolution now adopted as
they were for the Irish Land Act.

There is no cloaking the fact, that the position now
assumed is a retreat from that of Parnell in ’85, when,

“ Divorce Case ” and the Split wore outside his calcula-

—that the abnegation of ’85 would have been fully
justified by sucaass in '93. But the question is, would

the gentle soughing of the modern Irish Party. And
we are to-day, for good or ill, much nearer to the times
and circustandss of Butt than we should probably
care to admit.

It may be said that more skilful strategy might have
prever the Liberal Party from being committed
against Home Rule—as they are. It may be so. But
we must put up with such generalship as we have, and
deal with existing facts. Now it is an existing fact,
that the Liberals have pledged themselves so far against
conceding Home Rule in the pizsent Parliament that—
apart from any question of bad faith with their con-
stituents, a difficulty serious enough in itself—they have
given the House of Lords ample constitutional justifica-
tion for throwing out any mpasure of full Home Rule.
And seeing that the Irish Party seem likely to find them-
selves supporting the Lords against a majority of the
Commons on the Education Bill before the present year
is up, the right of that House to interfere in the
absence of a sufficient mandate is likely to receive a
very poignant exemplification. But the Lords may
equally throw out Devolution, it may be said. Devolu-
tion, however, is exactly on a par with all the other
measures in the Liberal programme, and if the Liberal
submit, to its being either rejected or emasculated b
amendment, they must be ready to see each particula
item in their programme from the Trades Disputes Bi
to the Welsh Disestablishment similarly mangled.
Whether they love Devolution or not, they must fight
for it or surrender all along the line.

But what is Devolution? A%t present the term has
no more defined meaning than “that which is not th
Union and is not Home Rule.”” It may be an instal-
ment, of 6d., it may be one of 15s. If you seek for a
closer definition you must look to the half-inspired
forecasts of the Daily Chronicle and other papers. M.
Swift MacNeill has, however, laid down a very sensible
standard of action for the Party in seeking to malke

It was also stated that the Gaelic League would work

training colleges, that boys wishing to become teachers

a disadvantage owing to tho form of education they

The district is one of the most picturesque and healthy

in Iroland. The sca-breezo and the mountain breeze

are there. The heather and the fern on the cliffs over-
hanging the ocean, and the wild thyme with its heavy
fragrance seom to promise bright cyes and faces
weather-brown to the palo weaklings of the city. It is
a grief to all like us who must wait for another year’s
span before we seo the long valley that opens east and
west glorified in the autumn sunset. What a difference
it would have made if, when we were young, we had
been taken away for two or three years from the dreary
streets of Dublin, and sent to such a place. But I
should not speak mercly of those things that meet the
eye. Can I and others like me ever measure the loss
we have sustained in never having had the opportunity
of attending such a school as this thirty or forty years
ago?! We might have learnt the Irish language in its
purest form with labour unfelt at that carly age, we
might have stored our minds with the folk-lore and the
legends which cover so much wisdom and old-world
knowledgoe under their simple garb, and we might have
won from the people a share in the most precious things
they have, that shrewd sense in the common dealings of
life and that keen eye of faith which sees in all things
the working of a higher power. Mmape.
